commit.message:
fix: Telegram "no response generated" — synthesis, person fields, calendar range (#44)

Three root causes for empty/wrong Telegram responses:

1. When the LLM exhausted all 5 tool rounds, the synthesis round ran
   without tools AND without any instruction to produce text. The LLM
   saw a conversation ending in tool results and produced nothing.
   Fix: add explicit synthesis instruction + fallback from tool results.

2. person_info omitted birthday, company, and position fields — data
   from Apple Contacts was fetched but never shown to the LLM.

3. Calendar search hardcoded ±30 days, missing recurring events like
   birthdays months away. Widened default to ±180 days.

Also: gitignore live e2e test file (contains real names for integration
tests against live server).

Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

commit.message:
feat: add JSR publishing support (#7253)

## Summary

- Add `jsr.json` config for all 18 publishable packages (everything
except the `mikro-orm` meta package)
- Add `scripts/publish-jsr.mjs` script that syncs versions from
`package.json` → `jsr.json` and publishes all packages
- Add `publish:jsr` / `publish:jsr:dry` scripts to root `package.json`
- Add JSR publish step to the release workflow (stable releases only,
after npm publish)
- Fix all 285 JSR "slow types" errors across all packages by adding
explicit return types and type annotations to public API surface

## Notes

- The `@mikro-orm` scope already exists on JSR — packages will be
created automatically on first publish
- Dynamic imports (`import(id)`) produce warnings (not errors) since
they can't be statically analyzed — these are intentional for runtime
driver loading
- JSR publishes TypeScript source directly, no build step needed
- Canary/RC builds are not published to JSR, only stable releases
(matching industry practice — Hono, Oak, Deno std all do the same)

## Test plan

- [x] `yarn build` passes
- [x] `yarn tsc-check-tests` passes
- [x] `yarn lint` passes (0 errors)
- [x] `yarn test` passes (5218 tests, 1064 files)
- [x] `yarn publish:jsr:dry` passes with 0 errors across all 18 packages
- [ ] First actual publish will happen with the v7 stable release

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

---------

Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

commit.message:
fix: webhook hardening — TCPA opt-out, message-status try/except, new-lead dedup

P0 — TCPA opt-out compliance:
- is_opt_out_message() now uses word-boundary matching (was exact-match only)
- Catches "stop please", "stop texting me", "stop it please" etc.
- Added "end" keyword to opt-out set
- GHL add_tag("opted-out-sms") called on opt-out detection

P0 — message-status endpoint:
- Wrapped handler in try/except; malformed JSON returns 200 not 500
- Prevents GHL retry storms on bad payloads

P0 — new-lead endpoint:
- Returns 200 {"status":"error"} on failures (not 500)
- HTTPException (401/400) still propagates correctly

P1 — reliability:
- New-lead: 60s dedup guard before analyze_lead
- Unified webhook: StallReengagementService reuses _webhook_cache (no Redis connection leak)
- Opted-out contacts: future messages return {"status":"skipped","reason":"opted_out"}

Tests: +25 new (1784 passing, 21 skipped)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Polish gradient map editor: handles, locking, undo, slider fixes

commit.message:
feat: Add dynamic JCross video analysis system

Implements video analysis where frame-to-frame changes are expressed as
dynamic JCross code modifications, inspired by ARC-AGI2 and human brain approaches.

Core Concept:
- Video frames → ARC-AGI2 grids (32x32, 10 colors)
- Grids → Cross structures
- Frame diffs → JCross code dynamic changes
- Change history → Cross layer mapping
- Code evolution → Observation & report

Implementation (Pure JCross):
- dynamic_video_analysis.jcross: Main analysis program
  1. Extract frames at 30fps
  2. Convert to ARC-AGI2 grids + Cross structures
  3. Detect frame-to-frame diffs
  4. Analyze changes and generate report

- dynamic_jcross_processors.py: Python processors (I/O only)
  - extract_frames: OpenCV video frame extraction
  - frame_to_grid: Frame → ARC-AGI2 grid (32x32, 10 colors)
  - grid_to_cross: Grid → Cross structure
  - calculate_diff: Detect changes between frames

- run_dynamic_video_analysis.py: Standalone runner

Design Document:
- DYNAMIC_JCROSS_VIDEO_ANALYSIS.md: Complete architecture
  - Frame decomposition at 30fps
  - Diff → JCross code generation
  - Change mapping to Cross layers
  - Code evolution observation
  - Report generation

ARC-AGI2 Assets Utilized:
- Grid representation (32x32)
- 10-color palette quantization
- Pattern transformation rules
- Human brain approach (differential memory)

Features:
- All logic in .jcross (Python only for I/O)
- ARC-AGI2 grid-based representation
- Frame diff detection
- Change type classification (added/removed/color-changed)
- Cross axis mapping:
  - FRONT: Point additions
  - BACK: Point removals
  - UP/DOWN: Attribute changes
  - RIGHT/LEFT: Point movements

Future Extensions (Documented):
- Full JCross code dynamic generation
- Cross layer change mapping
- Code evolution observation
- Detailed report generation

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Fix odds loading: use cached slate endpoint (instant from disk)

- Initial load hits /api/slate/cached/{sport} — reads from server disk, instant
- Server auto-falls back to live API if no cache exists
- Manual REFRESH still hits live /api/odds/{sport} for fresh data
- This is how it worked before when cards were dropping in fast

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix: route popup CV upload token through background ensureInstallToken

Popup was calling /api/register directly on every PDF/DOCX upload,
bypassing the shared token cache and mutex in background.js. This minted
a fresh orphaned token on every upload and could race if two uploads fired
concurrently.

Fix: add GET_TOKEN message handler in background.js that goes through
ensureInstallToken() (cached, mutex-protected). Popup now sends
{ type: 'GET_TOKEN' } and reuses the existing token.

commit.message:
Add Binance proxy endpoints to bypass geo-blocking

Binance Futures API (fapi.binance.com) is blocked in Indonesia due to
ISP TLS interception. All klines and 24hr ticker fetches fail with
net::ERR_CERT_CO / net::ERR_CONNECT errors in the browser.

Backend (running on Coolify/EU):
- Add GET /trading/proxy/klines — proxies Binance klines via httpx
- Add GET /trading/proxy/ticker24hr — proxies Binance 24hr ticker
- No auth required (public Binance data), input validated

Frontend:
- candlestick-chart.tsx: fetch klines from backend proxy instead of
  direct Binance REST URL
- use-market-data.ts: fetch 24hr ticker from backend proxy, remove
  unused BINANCE_REST_URL import

fix: add allow-all-egress policy to prevent default-deny lockout

egressDeny policies with endpointSelector: {} triggered Cilium's
default-deny for all egress, blocking API server, DNS, and pod-to-pod
traffic. Adding an explicit allow-all-egress policy restores default-allow
while deny rules still override for specific VLANs.

commit.message:
fix: disable autoCreate in production to fix MongoDB replica set connection

autoCreate: true conflicts with readPreference: secondaryPreferred —
MongoDB prohibits index/collection creation on secondary replica
connections. This was causing all database operations to fail with 500
errors in production.

commit.message:
Enable autonomous self-improvement: CEO can now evolve the company

The CEO agent now knows it can generate [Kassawat] goals that modify
the framework itself — adding agents, fixing workflows, improving
prompts, upgrading the pipeline. Evolution proposals are fed directly
into the CEO's context so self-improvement recommendations become
actionable goals.

The company can now see a problem (e.g. "developer keeps producing
truncated code"), generate a self-improvement goal, plan tasks to fix
its own agent prompts, execute them, review them, and deploy — all
without human intervention.
